coredata.dc:title: In-situ growth of nitrogen-doped carbonized polymer dots on black phosphorus for electrochemical DNA biosensor of Escherichia coli O157: H7
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Fan
Wang, Baoli
Yan, Lijun
Wang, Bei
Niu, Yanyan
Wang, Lisi
Sun, Wei
coredata.dc:description: Sensitive and accurate detection technology for pathogenic bacteria is of great social and economic significance in foodborne disease and food safety. In this paper, a novel portable electrochemical DNA biosensor for the detection of specific DNA sequence of Escherichia coli (E. coli) O157: H7 was constructed. To enhance the performance of the electrochemical sensor, a functionalized nitrogen-doped carbonized polymer dots in-situ grown on few-layer black phosphorus (N-CPDs@FLBP) was synthesized and used as the modifier on the surface of screen-printed electrode. Combining gold nanoparticles as immobilization matrix and methylene blue as electrochemical indicator, the analytical performance of this electrochemical DNA biosensor was evaluated using standard complementary ssDNA sequence in the linear concentration range from 1.0 × 10−19 to 1.0 × 10−6 mol/L with a low detection limit as 3.33 × 10−20 mol/L (3 σ). Furthermore, the portable electrochemical DNA biosensor was proposed based on polymerase chain reaction amplification for the detection of the E. coli O157: H7 genomic DNA from chicken meat, which verified the feasibility for practical samples detection. The research has great theoretical and practical significance for the development of electrochemical biosensor of pathogenic bacteria.

(a, b, e, f) TEM images and (c, d, g) HRTEM images of N-CPDs@FLBP, (inert of d: FFT image of N-CPDs@FLBP, the FFT image was obtained from surface regions of 10 × 10 nm2), (h) SAED image of N-CPDs@FLBP, (i-l) element mapping images of N-CPDs@FLBP.

(a) AFM image of N-CPDs@FLBP (inset is the corresponding height profiles), (b) XRD patterns of N-CPDs, FLBP and N-CPDs@FLBP, (c) Raman spectra of N-CPDs, FLBP and N-CPDs@FLBP, (d) the enlarged vibration region from FLBP and N-CPDs@FLBP.
(a) FT-IR patterns of N-CPDs@FLBP, FLBP, and N-CPDs; High-resolution XPS spectra: (b) P 2p spectrum of N-CPDs@FLBP, (c) C 1s spectra and (d) N 1s spectra of N-CPDs@FLBP and N-CPDs.

(a) EIS and (b) CV curves of different modified electrodes, cyclic stability with successive CV scans of 200 cycles on (c) NF/FLBP/SPE and (d) NF/N-CPDs@FLBP/SPE, The reduction peak currents of (e) NF/Au NPs/FLBP/SPE and (f) NF/Au NPs/N-CPDs@FLBP/SPE that stored at 4 °C for four weeks. (a) DPV responses of MB on the ssDNA/Au NPs/N-CPDs@FLBP/SPE hybridized with different concentrations of standard complementary target ssDNA from 1.0 × 10−19 to 1.0 × 10−6 mol/L, (b) Corresponding calibration curve (plot of I vs log C).

(a) DPV of MB on (curve a) Au NPs/N-CPDs@FLBP/SPE, (curve b) ssDNA/Au NPs/N-CPDs@FLBP/SPE and (curve c) hybridized with PCR product of specific E. coli O157: H7 genomic DNA and (b) the histogram of current, (c) Reproducibility on eight electrochemical DNA biosensor hybridized with 1.0 × 10−10 mol/L and 1.0 × 10−15 mol/L specific complementary target ssDNA sequence, (d) The change of peak current in 30 days storage in 4 °C refrigerator (I0 is the current of the first day).
(a) Synthesis of the N-CPDs@FLBP heterostructure by a one-step method; (b) Schematic representation of portable intelligent electrochemical DNA biosensor for the detection of E. coli O157: H7.

head.ce:title: In-situ growth of nitrogen-doped carbonized polymer dots on black phosphorus for electrochemical DNA biosensor of Escherichia coli O157: H7

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sensitive and accurate detection technology for pathogenic bacteria is of great social and economic significance in foodborne disease and food safety. In this paper, a novel portable electrochemical DNA biosensor for the detection of specific DNA sequence of Escherichia coli (E. coli) O157: H7 was constructed. To enhance the performance of the electrochemical sensor, a functionalized nitrogen-doped carbonized polymer dots in-situ grown on few-layer black phosphorus (N-CPDs@FLBP) was synthesized and used as the modifier on the surface of screen-printed electrode. Combining gold nanoparticles as immobilization matrix and methylene blue as electrochemical indicator, the analytical performance of this electrochemical DNA biosensor was evaluated using standard complementary ssDNA sequence in the linear concentration range from 1.0 × 10−19 to 1.0 × 10−6 mol/L with a low detection limit as 3.33 × 10−20 mol/L (3 σ). Furthermore, the portable electrochemical DNA biosensor was proposed based on polymerase chain reaction amplification for the detection of the E. coli O157: H7 genomic DNA from chicken meat, which verified the feasibility for practical samples detection. The research has great theoretical and practical significance for the development of electrochemical biosensor of pathogenic bacteria.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: As a global foodborne pathogen, Escherichia coli (E. coli) O157: H7 is a threat to public health and has been implicated in many outbreaks of haemorrhagic colitis [1]. Over the last twenty years, reports of foodborne illness due to the infection by E. coli O157: H7 from ingestion of contaminated meat, milk and leafy greens have also increased [2,3]. Therefore, to search a rapid, sensitive, portable and low-cost method for detecting this pathogen is now more important than ever. Conventional detection methods of E. coli O157: H7, such as polymerase chain reaction (PCR) and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) are labor-intensive and time-consuming with several steps, including selective culture, gram staining, and biochemical and/or serological tests [4,5]. The conversion of biological or biochemical responses into quantifiable optical, mechanical or electronic signals can be achieved by means of various kinds of bio-devices [6]. Most of these transducing elements can be miniaturized and incorporated into lab-on-a-chip devices. Xu et al. [7] built a micro optical sensors based on avalanching silicon light-emitting devices monolithically integrated on chips. In recent years, DNA biosensors have emerged as promising alternatives for faster, low-cost, and simpler methods of microbial detection, which use nucleic acids-based affinity detection of complementary target sequences [8].

To improve the sensing performance, a wide range of nanomaterials and strategy have been utilized for the development of electrochemical DNA biosensors [9,10]. For example, Arora et al. [11] reported a polyaniline-based DNA sensor detecting E. coli using methylene blue (MB) as redox hybridization indicator with the detection limit as 0.01 ng/µL. Rajapaksha et al. [12] described an approach for E. coli O157: H7 target detection via amperometric Au interdigitated electrode-based biosensor with the detection limit as 0.8 fmol/L. Many efforts have been developed in our group for the construction of high conductivity and large surface area for biomolecule immobilization with novel structured nanomaterials. For example, Au nanoparticles and graphene related composites were used for the electrochemical detection of staphylococcus nuc gene sequences with satisfactory results [13,14]. Also, Au nanoparticles and neutral red were grafted on the terminal of hairpin probe as signal tag for electrochemical DNA sensor [15]. Au-Pt bimetal decorated graphene modified electrodes were used for the detection of Vibrio parahaemolyticus specific tlh gene sequence [16].

As a newly studied 2D materials with large surface area, long charge carrier diffusion paths, relatively high carrier mobility, and lone-pairs, BP nanosheets are potential materials for electrochemical sensors [17–19]. Huang et al. summarized the recent applications of BP nanosheets in bioelectrochemistry including electrochemical sensors [17]. Niu et al. used BP modified glassy carbon electrode for the sensitive voltammetric detection of rutin [18]. However, because their lone pair of electrons on the surface and edges are highly reactive to form phosphate (PxOy) in ambient conditions, BP nanosheets suffer from intrinsic defects, which leads to ambient instability and restricts their applications [20].

0D-2D heterostructure has recently attracted extensive interests in tailoring the properties of 2D materials [21,22]. To optimize the properties of BP nanosheets, it is especially important to find another nanomaterial to covalently functionalize BP nanosheets, which can improve the chemical stability of phosphorene [23,24]. As a kind of burgeoning 0D carbon nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) with excellent biocompatibility, numerous electron-electron hole pairs, large specific surface area and low toxicity are considered to be promising material for biological and optoelectronic applications [25–28]. Apart from graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and carbon nanodots (CNDs), carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) are an emerging type of CDs with a carbonized carbon core and aggregated or crosslinked polymer chains/groups [29]. With a lot of polymer chains on the surface, CPDs possess better compatibility than GQDs, and excellent stability than polymers due to the carbonization [30]. Hydrothermal condensation crosslinking and carbonization is one primary bottom-up strategy to synthesize CDs with precursors of small molecules and/or polymers [31–33]. However, there are many difficulties in obtaining a 0D-2D heterostructure material with excellent properties. The synthesis process is relatively complex with weak linking between 0D and 2D materials. Besides, few reports on direct in-situ growth of 0D materials on BP nanosheets were published unit now.

Herein, a new 0D-2D heterostructure consisting of nitrogen-doped carbonized polymer dots (N-CPDs) directly in-situ growing on few-layer black phosphorus (FLBP) nanosheets is designed and realized by a one-step method. Additionally, a portable electrochemical DNA biosensor for detection of E. coli O157: H7 was constructed by modifying the as-prepared N-CPDs@FLBP on screen-printed electrode (SPE). The DNA biosensor is comprised of N-CPDs@FLBP modified SPE with gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) for thiolated single-stand probe DNA immobilization and methylene blue (MB) as indicator. SPE-based portable intelligent sensors have exhibited potential possibility for on-site detection because they can provide a miniaturized but robust and portable electrochemical detection system. In this paper, the correlation between current response and target ssDNA concentration is established by using DPV method, and smartphone with a specifically designed application is utilized to control the system. Finally, the DNA biosensor was tested for E. coli O157: H7 from the chicken meat to evaluate actual sample testing capability. Scheme 1 exhibited the preparation of N-CPDs@FLBP heterostructure and its usage in portable intelligent electrochemical DNA biosensing for E. coli O157: H7.

In section 2, the materials and methods used in this paper were described in detail. Firstly, materials (section 2.1) and instruments (section 2.2) that used in the experiments were listed and introduced. Then the synthesis procedure of N-CPDs@FLBP nanocomposite was explained (section 2.3). Thirdly, the fabrication of electrochemical DNA biosensor was demonstrated step-by-step in section 2.4, which was the basic for the further DNA analysis. Fourthly, the hybridization of target ssDNA sequences on the fabricated electrochemical DNA sensor was realized and further used for the realization of electrochemical detection with MB as indicator (section 2.5). Lastly, the real samples were treated to extract the DNA sequences, which was analyzed by the proposed procedure (section 2.6).

Black phosphorus (BP, purity > 99.998 %) and gold nanoparticles (Au NPs, 0.2 g/mL ethanol solution) were obtained from Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd. (Jiangsu, China). N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) and 0.5 % sodium dodecyl sulfate solution (SDS) were bought from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Nafion ethanol solution (NF, 5 wt%) was purchased from Beijing Honghaitian Tech. Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Methylene blue (MB) was obtained from Shanghai Chem. Plant (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ/cm) used in the experiment was filtered by Milli-Q IQ 7000 water purification system (Merck Millipore, Germany). Other chemicals were of analytical reagent grade.

All DNA oligonucleotides of E. coli O157: H7 were provided by Shanghai Sangon Biological Engineering Tech. Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), and the primer F and primer R were obtained from Tsingke Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China) with the sequences shown in Table 1 .

Electrochemical measurements were conducted on a portable electrochemical workstation (EmStat3 + Blue, Palmsens BV, Netherlands) connecting with a P40 Pro smart phone (Huawei Technologies Co., Ltd., China) through a three-electrode system of SPE (φ = 5 mm, Qingdao Poten Technology Co., Ltd., China). A P70D20TL-D4 household microwave oven (Output power 700 W, Guangdong Galanz Group Co., Ltd., China) was used to prepare the materials. Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images and selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern were obtained on a Tecnai G20 transmission electron microscope (FEI Co., USA). Scanning electron microscopic (SEM) images and energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) were recorded on JSM-7100F scanning electron microscope (Japan Electron Co., Japan) with an INCA X-act energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscope (Oxford Instruments, UK). Atomic force microscopic (AFM) images and data were performed using a Dimension FastScan atomic force microscope with Nanoscope V controller (Bruker, USA). Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) was recorded on a Nicolet 6700 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., USA). Raman was performed on a Horiba LabRAM HR Evolution Raman microscope (Horiba Scientific, France) using a 514 nm laser. X-ray diffraction (XRD) results were carried out on D8 Advance diffractometer with a Cu Kα radiation source (Bruker, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on the ESCALAB 250Xi XPS with laser Al Kα (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., USA). The pH of PBS was adjusted by Seven2Go pro portable pH/ion meter (Mettler Toledo Technology Co., Ltd., China).

Briefly, 25 mg BP powder was dispersed in 5 mL NMP to obtain a suspension, which was operated in microwave oven at medium-high for 8 min, subsequently heated at medium-low for 3 min. The yellow supernatant was obtained by centrifuging the solution at 7000 r/min for 20 min, which was named as N-CPDs@FLBP. Besides, contrast samples were prepared by changing the medium-high treatment times as 7, 9, 10 and 11 min with the following 3 min medium-low treatment, and the resulted samples were named as N-CPDs@FLBP-10, N-CPDs@FLBP-12, N-CPDs@FLBP-13, and N-CPDs@FLBP-14. To better discuss the mechanism, FLBP nanosheets were prepared by a similar procedure using ultrapure water as the solvent. N-CPDs with different microwave-assisted times were named as crosslinked polymer (CP), CP-6 to CP-8, and N-CPDs-9 to N-CPDs-13. It’s worth mentioning that these samples need to be protected from light and stored at −20 °C.

SPE was directly employed as substrate electrode and the DNA modified electrode was prepared based on the similar procedure of our previous work [14]. Firstly, 8 μL N-CPDs@FLBP solution was dropped on SPE and dried at room temperature. Then, 10 μL 0.2 g/mL Au NPs (particle size 40 nm, Fig. S1) ethanol solution that mixed with 0.5 wt% Nafion ethanol solution was further dropped and dried to obtain the Au NPs/N-CPDs@FLBP/SPE. Afterward, the thiolated ssDNA probe sequence immobilization process was carried out by dropping 10 μL 1.0 × 10−7 mol/L thiolated ssDNA probe solution on Au NPs/N-CPDs@FLBP/SPE and incubated at room temperature for 1.0 h. Since Au NPs has an affinity to thiolate functional groups, the probe ssDNA self-assembled monolayers could be covalently fixed on the electrode surface with the formation of AuS bonds. Afterward, 0.5 % SDS solution and PBS were used to rinse and remove unbound probe ssNDA to obtain ssDNA/Au NPs/N-CPDs@FLBP/SPE. The preparation procedure of the modified electrodes was performed in the glove box under N2 protection.

Briefly, 10.0 μL standard complementary target ssDNA sequences with the concentrations from 1.0 × 10−19 mol/L to 1.0 × 10−6 mol/L were dropped on the electrode surface to hybridize with immobilized probe ssDNA and kept 30 min for incubation process. Then, 0.5 % SDS solution and PBS were used to wash and remove the unbounded target ssDNA to obtain the dsDNA/Au NPs/N-CPDs@FLBP/SPE. For the MB-binding process, dsDNA/Au NPs/N-CPDs@FLBP/SPE was immersed in 6.0 × 10−4 mol/L MB solution for 8 min and took out. The excess MB was washed thoroughly with 0.5 % SDS and PBS to make sure that the obtained MB was only bound in the DNA sequences on the electrode. DPV measurements were performed for the hybridization with different concentrations target ssDNA sequences in 0.05 mol/L PBS and the parameters were set as: pulse width of 0.05 s, pulse period of 0.2 s, and pulse amplitude of 0.008 V.

DNA samples were extracted from the chicken meat (obtained from Guilinyang market, Haikou, China) using animal tissue DNA extraction kits (Xi'an Tianlong Science and Technology Co., Ltd., China). The spoiled chicken meat was cut into pieces and placed into a tube with 10 mL concentrated digestive juice, then the tube was kept in a 25 °C shaker and incubated for 48 h. The obtained digestive solution was centrifuged at 12000 rpm/min for 4 min and the upper layer was removed. The DNA was precipitated and dissolved in 100 μL sterile ultrapure water. In a final volume of 25 μL amplification mixture 200.0 nmol/L Primer F and Primer R, 2.5 μL 10 × reaction set B, 2.0 mmol/L MgCl2, 200.0 nmol/L triphosphate deoxynucleoside (dGTP, dATP, dCTP, dTTP), 1.0 μL template DNA sequence and 1.5 U Taq DNA polymerase (Promega, Wisconsin USA) are contained. Briefly, the following temperature program was applied: a) initiation step, 94 °C for 30 s, b) second step, 35 cycles amplification for primer consisting of 5 min of 72 °C for extension, and c) final step, cool down and kept at 4 °C. DPV measurements were performed for the obtained amplified product in 0.05 mol/L PBS.

In section 3, the experimental data and discussions of the results were present in detail. Firstly, the nanomaterials synthesized were carefully characterized by different techniques including TEM, AFM, XRD, Raman, FT-IR and XPS with the explanations listed (section 3.l). Then the electrochemical behaviors of the nanocomposite modified electrodes were investigated by different electrochemical methods such as CV, EIS and DPV, which proved the electrochemical properties of the fabricated modified electrodes (section 3.2 and section 3.3). Lastly, the analytical performances of the designed electrochemical DNA sensor were investigated to the construction of the working curves for the target ssDNA sequences (section 3.4), and further used to analyze the real chicken meat samples with the reproducibility and lifetime checked (section 3.5).

As presented in Fig. 1 , N-CPDs@FLBP heterostructure can be rapidly synthesized by one step microwave-assisted method in bulk BP solution with NMP as solvent by combining both “top-down” and “bottom-up” approaches [31,34]. With the help of medium-high power microwave irradiation, van der Waals interaction between the bulk BP flakes starts weakening and further exfoliating layer by layer (top-down procedure) [35]. Afterwards, medium-low power microwave irradiation is used to further exfoliate and separate BP into FLBP nanosheets. Simultaneously, NMP with groups of CN and CO begins to dehydrate, polymerize, and carbonize to form N-CPDs under the microwave irradiation (bottom-up procedure) [36]. The total microwave time is optimized as 11 min with 8 min in medium-high power microwave irradiation and 3 min medium-low power microwave irradiation, which gave a deepest yellow solution (Fig. S2). When the time is shorter than 11 min, the layers of FLBP in the N-CPDs@FLBP composites may be thicker, and if the time is longer than 11 min, the particle sizes of N-CPDs in N-CPDs@FLBP composites are too small with the decreasing of polymer chains/groups.

TEM images of N-CPDs@FLBP heterostructure (Fig. 2 a-g) display that numerous N-CPDs with diameters from 2 to 5 nm (Fig. 2f) evenly disperse on FLBP nanosheets. The fast fourier transform (FFT) image of N-CPDs@FLBP (inset of Fig. 2d) confirms a poly-crystalline structure. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of N-CPDs@FLBP shows the characteristic spacings of 0.26 nm and 0.42 nm for the (220) and (111) lattice planes of BP (Fig. 2d). Overlapping with FLBP, the N-CPDs with lattice spacings of 0.18 nm, 0.20 nm and 0.21 nm can be attributed to the (102), (101) and (100) planes of graphite (Fig. 2c, d, and g), respectively. However, the SAED image of N-CPDs@FLBP shows an amorphous structure due to the degree of crystallinity (Fig. 2h). Meanwhile, P, C and N elements can be observed in the EDS mappings of N-CPDs@FLBP heterostructure (Fig. 2i-l). All these results can prove the successful composition of FLBP and N-CPDs. In order to better verify the mechanism, SEM images of bulk BP and N-CPDs@FLBP prepared at different microwave-assisted time from 10 min to 14 min (Fig. S3), and TEM images of crosslinked polymer (CP) and N-CPDs with different microwave time were recorded (Fig. S4). Overall, these results further support the previous mechanism of polymer/carbon hybrid structure in Fig. 1.

AFM was further carried out to investigate the 2D (Fig. 3 a) and 3D (Fig. S5) morphology of N-CPDs@FLBP with the images and the corresponding height profiles present (inset in Fig. 3a). As previously reported, the thickness of theoretical single layer BP is approximate 0.6 nm [37]. According to the statistical AFM analysis of N-CPDs@FLBP (inset in Fig. 3a), the average thickness was 2.54 ± 0.86 nm, corresponding to approximately 3 to 5 layers of single layer BP.

Typical XRD patterns of the N-CPDs, FLBP and N-CPDs@FLBP are shown in Fig. 3b. N-CPDs have a wide scattering peak at 2θ = 26° due to the introduction of lots of disordered polymer chains on the surface of carbon skeleton, which corresponds to the (002) plane of graphite (JCPDS, card No. 75-1621) [38]. This result supports the polymer/carbon hybrid structure of N-CPDs. A broad peak at 20–24° in the XRD pattern of FLBP is considered as a (111) typical plane of primitive cubic BP (JCPDS, card No. 02-0266). The gaps between the three cases are uniform, so N-CPDs and FLBP can be clearly distinguished by this phenomenon. Besides, it can further indicate the successful preparation of N-CPDs@FLBP heterostructure. The peak of N-CPDs@FLBP has shifted to 18°, which indicates that there are bonds between N-CPDs and FLBP, and the corresponding interlayer spacing in N-CPDs@FLBP becomes smaller [39]. This smaller corresponding interlayer spacing also shows FLBP in the N-CPDs@FLBP heterostructure becomes more stable.

Raman spectra were studied to evaluate the influence of the formation of PC/POC bonds on the oscillation of BP in N-CPDs@FLBP. Typical Raman results are common to graphene with D band at 1364 cm−1 and G band at 1580 cm−1 (Fig. 3c) [40,41]. The appearance of D and G peaks in N-CPDs@FLBP also testifies the successful synthesis of the composite. Typically, a schematic diagram of the vibration modes of three characteristic Raman peaks of Ag 1, B2g and Ag 2 can be obviously observed in FLBP and N-CPDs@FLBP. It has been proved that the integrated ratio of Ag 1/Ag 2 as a function of layer thickness highly depends on the oxidation degradation of BP [39,42]. Thus, the Ag 1/Ag 2 intensity ratio of FLBP and N-CPDs@FLBP were analyzed with the resultant histogram plotted in Fig. S6. The Ag 1/Ag 2 ratio of N-CPDs@FLBP (0.356) locates in the region of 0.2 to 0.6, indicating that N-CPDs@FLBP with thinner thickness has a lower oxidation level than that of FLBP (0.799). As compared with that of FLBP, three characteristic vibration peaks Ag 1, B2g and Ag 2 of N-CPDs@FLBP move 3.2 cm−1, 6.2 cm−1 and 6.4 cm−1 to the lower wavenumber, respectively (Fig. 3d). It has been reported that the Raman characteristic peak of BP moves to the high wavenumber along with the enhancement of the surface phosphorous atom oscillation, and to the low wavenumber along with the weakening of the phosphorus atom oscillation [39]. Therefore, the shift of three characteristic peaks to the lower waven umber in the Raman spectrum of N-CPDs@FLBP means that the PC/POC bonds between FLBP and N-CPDs weaken the oscillation of phosphorus atom and thus stabilize FLBP [43].

The chemical structural information of N-CPDs@FLBP was also investigated by FT-IR (Fig. 4 a). The broad bands at 3455 cm−1 and 2932 cm−1 can be corresponded to the OH/NH and CH stretching modes, respectively. Besides, peaks at 1507 cm−1, 1415 cm−1 and 1100 cm−1 belong to the CC stretching pattern of the polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, and others at 1259 cm−1 and 1298 cm−1 can be ascribed to the stretching vibrations of COC and asymmetric tensile vibrations of C-NH-C. These results confirm that N-CPDs are polycyclic aromatic structure with aromatic CN species coated with polymer, which are rich with carboxyl, amide and hydroxyl groups [44], and can enhance the hydrophilia of the material. The feature peaks at 980 cm−1 and 930 cm−1 can be attributed to PO as well as POC bonds, whereas the additional vibration band at 850 cm−1 can be most likely associated with the PC bonds [43]. Overall, all the characteristic peaks of N-CPDs can be observed in that of N-CPDs@FLBP, which further confirms that N-CPDs successfully grown on the surface of FLBP by in-situ synthetic method. Besides, FT-IR spectra of N-CPDs obtained at different microwave-assisted time were studied (Fig. S7), which showed the increased characteristic peaks of polymer chains/groups.

XPS was further characterized to systematically investigate the chemical composition and surface states of N-CPDs@FLBP. Compared to the full spectrum of N-CPDs with C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s (Fig. S8), the survey spectrum of N-CPDs@FLBP shows the typical peak of P 2p, C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, indicating the existence of FLBP (Fig. S8). The high-resolution XPS spectrum (P 2p) for N-CPDs@FLBP presents doublet characteristic peaks of crystalline BP at 127.9 eV of P 2p3/2 and 129.6 eV of P 2p1/2, respectively (Fig. 4b), and the peak at 133.6 eV is attributed to the presence of PC bonds in the N-CPDs@FLBP composite [45]. The broad sub-bond at 135.5 eV is observed, which could be assigned to the oxidized phosphorus (PxOy) on the surface of the degraded BP flakes [46]. The additional signal at 131.2 eV close to P 2p1/2 could also confirm the existence of POC bonds between FLBP and N-CPDs [45,47]. Besides, O 1s of N-CPDs@FLBP has bridging phosphorus-oxygen PO (531.4 eV) and POP (532.2 eV) components assigned [48]. While O 1s of N-CPDs has CO (531.7 eV) and CO (533.0 eV) components assigned (Fig. S8). The five signals of C 1s of the N-CPDs@FLBP (Fig. 4c) are attributed to PC (283.2 eV), CC/CC (284.8 eV), CN (285.7 eV), COC (286.6 eV) and OCO (288.8 eV) functional groups, respectively [38,49]. The C 1s spectrum of N-CPDs shows the absence of PC bond. Moreover, both the N 1s spectra of N-CPDs@FLBP and N-CPDs (Fig. 4d) reveal three relative nitrogen species of CNC (398.2 eV) in pyridinic-N, N-C3 (400.5 eV) from quaternary-N, and NH (402.6 eV) from graphitic-N [50]. Furthermore, the electron density in the carbon layers can be effectively increased by pyridinic-N and graphitic-N with lone-pair electrons [36]. On the basis of the above results, PC bonds and POC bonds are formed between FLBP and N-CPDs, which can improve the stability of FLBP. The material with this structure may also be used in metal-oxide semiconductor-like device, which could enhance the electric-optical signal response in the bio-optical sensing systems [51].

Cyclic stability with 200 cycles continuous CV scans indicates that NF/FLBP/SPE presents observable changes (Fig. 5c), while NF/N-CPDs@FLBP/SPE remains almost unchanged (Fig. 5d). Relative standard deviations (RSDs) of NF/FLBP/SPE with both oxidation and reduction peak currents are 5.27 % and 3.62 % (Fig. 5c), respectively. Meanwhile, the RSDs of NF/N-CPDs@FLBP/SPE are 1.15 % and 1.03 % (Fig. 5d), indicating that NF/N-CPDs@FLBP/SPE presents better electrochemical cyclic stability. NF/Au NPs/FLBP/SPE and NF/Au NPs/N-CPDs@FLBP/SPE are stored at 4 °C in moist air environment and tested by CV during 4 weeks. The reduction peak currents on NF/Au NPs/FLBP/SPE maintained 56.4 % of its initial value (Fig. 5e), while that of NF/Au NPs/N-CPDs@FLBP/SPE was 87.3 % (Fig. 5f). These results demonstrate the excellent environmental stability and long lifetime of NF/Au NPs/N-CPDs@FLBP/SPE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DPV responses of MB on different modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: MB is often used as an hybridization indicator to distinguish between ssDNA and dsDNA for electrochemical DNA biosensors [52]. The concentration and accumulation time of MB solution were optimized by DPV method (Fig. 6 a and 6b). Notably, the current response increased gradually with the increment of the concentration of MB due to the more MB accumulated on ssDNA/Au NPs/N-CPDs@FLBP/SPE with a maximal value at the concentration of 6.0 × 10−4 mol/L. The influence of accumulation time of 6.0 × 10−4 mol/L MB solution was tested from 2 min to 12 min. The electrochemical response of MB had an increasing tendency up to 8 min without further improvement. Therefore 8 min was the optimal time of MB accumulation.

Furthermore, Fig. 6c exhibits the current responses of different modified electrodes. The reduction peak current of MB on ssDNA/Au NPs/SPE (curve b) was higher than that of ssDNA/SPE (curve a), which could be attributed to Au NPs with high combining capacity to bond the thiolate functional groups of ssDNA. The presence of probe ssDNA sequence can further accumulate more MB due to the interaction with the guanine group of probe ssDNA on the electrode surface. Enhanced electron transfer and the significantly increased reduction peak were achieved by introduction of N-CPDs or FLBP into the sensing interface (curves c and d), owing to numerous electron-electron hole pairs of N-CPDs to provide more transmission path, and large surface area, high carrier mobility and excellent molecular adsorption performance of FLBP. In the case of ssDNA/Au NPs/N-CPDs@FLBP/SPE (curve e), it displays superior electrochemical performance over ssDNA/Au NPs/SPE (curve b), ssDNA/Au NPs/N-CPDs/SPE (curve c) and ssDNA/Au NPs/FLBP/SPE (curve d), which is mainly attributed to the synergistic effects of Au NPs and N-CPDs@FLBP. These results further indicate that the heterostructure of N-CPDs@FLBP could promote the electron transfer with good biocompatibility.

In addition, the selectivity of the proposed electrochemical DNA biosensor was evaluated using the ssDNA/Au NPs/N-CPDs@FLBP/SPE to hybridize with different sorts of ssDNA sequences (Fig. 6d). The current for the ssDNA/Au NPs/N-CPDs@FLBP/SPE (curve a) was the largest one, whereas the complementary sequence (curve e) had the lowest one. The high current of ssDNA/Au NPs/N-CPDs@FLBP/SPE can be attributed to the bonding of exposed guanine residues in the ssDNA sequence with MB. Nevertheless, the formation of hybrid dsDNA structures on the electrode could inhibit the interactions of MB with guanine residues. Therefore, the current signal changes of MB enabled us to accurately distinguish between probe ssDNA and the hybrid dsDNA. Besides, the current signal changes of the probe ssDNA modified electrode hybridized with non-complementary sequence (curve b), three-base mismatched ssDNA sequence (curve c) and one-base mismatched ssDNA sequence (curve d) decreased gradually, indicating the partly formation of dsDNA sequences after hybridization. The current decreases greatly with 66.70 % degree when ssDNA/Au NPs/N-CPDs@FLBP/SPE hybridizes with specific complementary target ssDNA sequence (curve e). The results show a good selectivity toward the detection of the complementary oligonucleotides of E. coli O157: H7.

To investigate the sensitivity of the designed electrochemical DNA biosensor, DPV was performed on different concentrations of standard complementary target ssDNA sequence (Fig. 7 a). The DPV current signals decreased with increasing target ssDNA concentrations from 1.0 × 10−19 to 1.0 × 10−6 mol/L, indicating that the more dsDNA formed on the electrode surface, the less MB interacted with guanine residues on the electrode. When the reduction current of MB did not decrease and tended to a stable value, indicating that all probe ssDNA sequences on the electrode surface were involved in the hybridization reaction. The formation of dsDNA on the electrode leads to less guanine base for MB to interact, so there are fewer MB molecules on the electrode with the decrease of electrochemical response of MB. Besides, Fig. 7b shows that the reduction peak is linearly proportional to the concentrations of target ssDNA in the range of 1.0 × 10−19 to 1.0 × 10−6 mol/L with the linear regression equation as I (μA) = 23.67 + 9.84 logC, R2 = 0.993), and the detection limit is calculated to be 3.33 × 10−20 mol/L (3 σ). Comparations with other electrochemical sensors about the detection of E. coli O157: H7 were listed in Table 2 , which indicated that the presented sensor exhibited a lower detection limit and a wider liner range. The results may be profited from the unique N-CPDs@FLBP heterostructure that produced a strong interfacial force with Au NPs and further provided abundant electron channels for the reduction of MB.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real sample analysis, reproducibility and lifetime
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed electrochemical DNA biosensor was employed for the detection of genomic DNA of E. coli O157: H7 based on PCR amplification products from chicken meat to verify the feasibility of practical application (Fig. 8 a, b). The electrochemical responses of MB with Au NPs/N-CPDs@FLBP/SPE (Fig. 8a, curve a), ssDNA/Au NPs/N-CPDs@FLBP/SPE (curve b) and the electrode hybridized with PCR product of genomic DNA of E. coli O157: H7 from spoiled chicken meat (curve c) were recorded. Obviously, the response greatly decreased on curve c, which indicated the hybridization with target ssDNA in sample and the ssDNA/Au NPs/N-CPDs@FLBP/SPE could realize the detection of target ssDNA sequence in a real sample solution from PCR treatment.

Reproducibility and lifetime studies were conducted on the electrochemical DNA biosensor. Fig. 8c shows good reproducibility on eight modified electrodes with RSD values of 4.03 % and 2.60 % in two different concentrations of specific complementary target ssDNA sequence (1.0 × 10−10 mol/L and 1.0 × 10−15 mol/L). To evaluate the lifetime of this biosensor, the peak current was recorded during 30 days with the storage in 4 °C refrigerator (Fig. 8d), which indicated that the response did not suggest a substantial difference for storage time of 15 days to the first day with current remaining 96.83 % of the initial response. From 30 days, the response decreased 20.13 % as compared to the first day, suggesting a relative long-term stability.

Sensitive and accurate detection methods have always been a challenge in the field of food safety testing. Aiming at this demand, this paper designed and synthesized a 0D-2D heterostructure consisting of 0D N-CPDs on 2D FLBP nanosheets. Furthermore, a portable intelligent electrochemical DNA biosensor was established using this composite as the modifier on SPE to achieve sensitive and accurate detection of E. coli O157: H7 with Au NPs as immobilization matrix and MB as electrochemical indicator, respectively. The correlation between current response and target ssDNA sequence concentration is established by DPV method with wider linear range (1.0 × 10−19 to 1.0 × 10−6 mol/L) and lower detection limit (3.33 × 10−20 mol/L, 3 σ). Finally, this designed electrochemical DNA biosensor presents a perfect platform for detecting the E. coli O157: H7 samples from chicken meat combined with a portable electrochemical workstation. The distinct advantages of the proposed sensing platform, such as high specificity and sensitivity, and good reproducibility, make it a potential platform for detecting E. coli O157: H7 in related food safety and clinical diagnosis.
